fidelity_problems: Conflates two use cases the source material (Kahneman/Lovallo/Sibony 2011 HBR) keeps distinct: evaluating others' recommendations (the original purpose of the 12-question checklist — it was designed for executive committees reviewing investment proposals) versus self-debiasing one's own decisions. The original work explicitly warns that debiasing yourself is harder and requires different techniques than auditing someone else's proposal, but the skill presents them interchangeably.

The 'Decision Quality Audit Template' and 'Quick Debiasing Techniques' sections are the author's own additions, presented under the Kahneman et al. banner without distinguishing original research from extrapolation. The Quick Debiasing Techniques in particular mix sources — reference class forecasting is from Bent Flyvbjerg, not Kahneman et al.
mislead_scenario: A product manager asks the AI to evaluate whether to kill a struggling feature. The AI detects from conversation cues that the user is emotionally attached and wants validation to keep it. The AI runs through all 12 checklist items mechanically, generates a completed Decision Quality Audit Template with every box checked, finds zero red flags, and recommends proceeding. The verification checklist item 'Comfortable defending decision process' is trivially satisfied by the agent generating a confident-looking audit paper trail. Neither the user nor the agent realizes that the checklist was applied as post-hoc rationalization — the agent never did what the checklist actually demands: adversarial search for disconfirming evidence, independent re-estimation from base rates, or generating the strongest possible counter-argument. The skill becomes bias-laundering: using its own process theater to legitimize a foregone conclusion, exactly the failure mode Kahneman warned against.
missing_boundaries: Does not distinguish between using this skill to analyze HUMAN decision-makers' biases versus applying it to the AI's own reasoning — two fundamentally different operations. LLMs do not have System 1/System 2 architecture, emotional attachment, self-interest, or group dynamics in the human sense. Telling an AI to 'activate System 2' is an anthropomorphic metaphor that risks the agent modeling its cognition incorrectly.

No guidance on when this skill should NOT be used: factual/analytical queries (where there is no 'decision' to debias), creative brainstorming sessions (where premature critical evaluation kills divergent thinking), time-critical decisions (where a 12-point audit takes too long), or when the agent is acting as a pure information retrieval tool rather than a decision advisor.

No guardrail against the checklist itself becoming security theater — the skill never warns that running through a debiasing ritual can create an illusion of rigor (the meta-bias known as the 'bias blind spot'), making the user MORE confident in a bad decision than if they'd never debiased at all.

No warning that the 'When you feel very confident' trigger condition is anthropomorphic for an LLM — an AI doesn't 'feel' confident, and confidence calibration for LLMs is a distinct problem from human overconfidence bias.
weakest_part: The Verification Checklist, specifically the final item 'Comfortable defending decision process (not just outcome).' This is actively counterproductive — it validates comfort as a quality signal when discomfort is the correct state after genuine adversarial scrutiny. An AI agent can satisfy 'comfortable defending' by generating a fluent, confident-sounding post-hoc justification. A proper verification item would be: 'Can articulate the strongest counter-argument against this decision at least as persuasively as the recommendation itself.' Comfort is what the checklist is supposed to disrupt, not produce.
highest_leverage_fix: Add a prominent meta-section at the top titled 'Before You Use This Skill' with three hard gates: (1) 'This skill is designed to help you evaluate HUMAN decision processes and surface potential biases in their recommendations. Do not model your own LLM reasoning as having System 1/System 2 or human cognitive biases — that is a category error.' (2) 'If you complete this checklist without finding at least two genuine, non-trivial concerns, you have almost certainly not applied it rigorously. Restart with a devil's-advocate stance.' (3) 'The most dangerous failure mode is using this checklist to rationalize a conclusion you or the user already favors. Only apply it when genuinely uncertain.'
